data.title: Acme United outlines $6M-$7M 2026 capex as tariff impact is expected to lessen over next 3 quarters
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Acme United (ACU) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Acme United had a difficult first quarter of 2026,” said (Chairman and CEO Walter Johnsen), adding: “While our net sales increased 14% to $52.3 million, our net income was $985,000 compared to $1.6 million last year, and earnings per share were $0.24 compared to $0.41 last year.”

  • On the My Medic acquisition, (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) said: “We purchased My Medic for $18.6 million during the first quarter of 2026,” and described the model as “cyclical with most of the profits generated in the fourth quarter of the year.” He added: “Our sales increase of 14% in the first quarter of 2026 includes approximately 8% from My Medic, which was at breakeven in P&L.”

  • On margins and tariffs, (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) said: “The company's gross margins in the first quarter of 2026 were 39.7% compared to 39% last year,” but “when the impact of the high gross margins at My Medic are removed, the core gross margins declined due to higher costs and tariffs.” He added: “We expect to run through these items during the second quarter with a return to normal levels in the third quarter.”

  • On inventory actions tied to geopolitical risk, (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) said: “Shortly after the war in Iran began, we started purchasing higher-than-normal quantities of raw materials and finished goods inventory,” and “so far, we have purchased approximately $10 million of incremental inventory.”

  • On operations and growth areas, (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) said: “We are completing the move into our new Spill Magic facility in Mt. Pleasant, Tennessee,” adding: “Orders for the business are strong, and we are experiencing record growth.” He also said European performance included that “sales increased 19% in local currency to EUR 4 million” and that the Schmiedeglut acquisition “is exceeding expectations.”

  • CFO summary (VP, CFO, Secretary & Treasurer Paul Driscoll): “SG&A expenses for the first quarter of 2026 were $19 million or 36% of net sales compared with $15.5 million or 34% of net sales for the same period of 2025,” and “net debt increased from $27.2 million at March 31, 2025, to $38.6 million at March 31, 2026.”

Outlook

  • (VP, CFO Driscoll) described the duration of tariff pressure, saying: “We expect the tariff impact to gradually lessen over the next 3 quarters as the tariff rate declined in November 2025 and again in February 2026.”

  • (VP, CFO Driscoll) flagged a cost headwind expected to fade, stating: “Additionally, the incremental cost to enhance the quality assurance protocols at the Med-Nap facility will not repeat in the second quarter of 2026.”

  • Compared with the prior call’s broader growth framing, (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) reiterated a promotion recovery setup for Westcott: “The big part coming back is really second, third, fourth quarters where last year, we had no promotions,” and “we should be showing growth.”

Financial Results

  • (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) reported: “Our net sales increased 14% to $52.3 million,” and “revenues, excluding My Medic, increased 6%.”

  • (VP, CFO Driscoll) attributed profit pressure to specific drivers: “Net income for the first quarter of 2026 was $1 million or $0.24 per diluted share,” and “the decline in net income was primarily due to the higher tariff and Med-Nap costs we experienced in the first quarter of this year.”

  • (VP, CFO Driscoll) linked mix and cost effects in gross margin: “The gross margin was 39.7%,” and “the favorable mix from higher-margin direct-to-consumer My Medic products was mostly offset by the impact of increased tariffs.”

  • (VP, CFO Driscoll) detailed cash uses and sources over the last year: “During the 12-month period ended March 31, 2026, we paid $14.6 million for the acquisition of the assets of My Medic, distributed approximately $2.4 million in dividends and purchased the cutting and sharpening line of products in Germany for $1.6 million,” and “we generated approximately $14.2 million in free cash flow before the purchase of a new $6 million manufacturing and distribution facility in Tennessee.”

Q&A

  • Richard Dearnley, Longport Partners, L.P.: “Could you put a dollar amount… on what the quality assurance protocols are involving?” (Chairman and CEO Johnsen): “About $300,000” in Q1 and “about $1.250 million or $1.3 million” in total so far; (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) added the facility is “about 3 quarters done” and called it “an investment.”

  • Richard Dearnley, Longport Partners, L.P.: asked if DTC know-how transfers across Acme. (Chairman and CEO Johnsen): “My Medic… is our first step in the United States to do direct-to-consumer,” and “we have 0.5 million social media followers today.”

  • Timothy Call, The Capital Management Corporation: asked if recent actions can add to “long-term sales, margins and earnings growth.” (Chairman and CEO Johnsen): “Tim, yes, we certainly do,” and said Spill Magic growth was strong, noting: “This quarter… it was up… over 30%.”

  • Georgy Vashchenko, Freedom Broker: asked if cutting/sharpening trends recovered. (Chairman and CEO Johnsen): “Westcott was down 2%,” and said promotions should support improvement in later quarters: “We have easy comparisons… in the second, third, and fourth quarter.”

  • Jake Patterson, Talanta Investment Group: asked about CapEx. (VP, CFO Driscoll): “I think we're looking at about $6 million -- probably $7 million.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on cost visibility and normalization (e.g., “Could you put a dollar amount…,” “Is that kind of a fair run rate…,” “Did they recover?”).

  • Management tone was slightly negative in prepared remarks but more confident on normalization and investment payoffs, with (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) saying: “Acme United had a difficult first quarter,” while also stating: “we certainly do” (on long-term benefits) and outlining timing: “return to normal levels in the third quarter.”

  • Versus the prior quarter’s upbeat framing (“record sales and earnings in 2025”), the current call carried more near-term defensiveness on tariffs and costs, while keeping confidence language around preparedness (“we are planning and acting”) and easing pressures (“tariff impact… lessen”).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The narrative shifted from Q4’s “record sales and earnings in 2025” and acquisition introduction to Q1’s integration-and-cost digestion, including My Medic’s seasonality and ad intensity.

  • Tariffs remained central in both calls, but Q1 emphasized P&L timing and drawdown, with (VP, CFO Driscoll) describing inventory capitalization and that the company “started to realize the full impact to earnings” in Q1, alongside a defined easing window (“over the next 3 quarters”).

  • Analyst focus broadened from deal structure/integration in Q4 to cost quantification, margin/tariff math, and segment recovery in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • (VP, CFO Driscoll) cited “continued high inflation, high interest rates and the imposition of new tariffs or changes in existing tariff rates,” and said “we may experience” supply chain disruptions again.

  • (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) described conflict-driven supply risk actions: “we… purchased approximately $10 million of incremental inventory,” adding: “we are planning and acting to be prepared for increasing costs and shortages.”

  • On regulatory and quality systems, (Chairman and CEO Johnsen) said the FDA “found a number of deficiencies” in documentation and equipment qualification at Brooksville, and management response included hiring a consulting firm to “upgrade the entire facility.”

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a tariff- and cost-pressured quarter despite higher sales, while emphasizing that tariff drag is expected to fade over the next three quarters, Med-Nap quality-assurance spending is expected not to repeat in Q2, and operational investments—especially Spill Magic’s Tennessee facility, automation, and My Medic integration—are positioned to support improved performance later in 2026, with planned 2026 CapEx of about $6 million to $7 million.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pulte reaffirms 2026 closings of 28,500-29,000 and projects $5.4B land spend amid elevated incentives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PulteGroup (PHM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "In a quarter that grew increasingly more complicated, you delivered exceptional results, both operationally and financially" (President, CEO & Director Ryan Marshall), adding that "our $3.3 billion in home sale revenues, 24.4% gross margins and lower share count all contributed to driving earnings of $1.79 per share" and that the company "invest[ed] $1.3 billion in land acquisition and development, while returning $360 million to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends."
  • "Build-to-order homes accounted for 43% of net new orders, up from 40% in Q1 of last year" (President, CEO & Director Marshall), and "we ended the quarter with an average of 1.4 finished specs per community, which is inside our target range of 1 to 1.5 finished specs per community."
  • "Incentives in the quarter reached 10.9% of gross sales price" (President, CEO & Director Marshall), while "we started approximately 6,500 homes against orders of 8,000 homes in the quarter" and "during the first few weeks of April, demand conditions have remained on track with typical seasonal trends."
  • "We ended the first quarter with 229,000 lots under control" (Executive VP & CFO James Ossowski) and "in a separate press release we issued this morning, we announced that our Board authorized an additional $1.5 billion for share repurchases, which brings total availability to $2.1 billion."

Outlook

  • "We expect to close between 6,700 and 7,100 homes in the second quarter of 2026" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), and "this keeps us on track with our previous guidance on closings in the range of 28,500 to 29,000 homes for full year 2026."
  • "We expect the average sales price of second quarter closings to be in the range of $540,000 to $550,000" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), and "for the full year 2026, we reaffirm our previous guidance of ASP of $550,000 to $560,000."
  • "We expect second quarter gross margin to be in the range of 24.1% to 24.4%" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), adding, "we expect Q2 gross margins to be the low point for 2026" and "we maintain our guide for full year 2026 gross margin to be in the range of 24.5% to 25.0%, although likely towards the lower end of the range."
  • Change vs. prior quarter guidance language: "This keeps us on track with our previous guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski) and "we reaffirm our previous guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski) contrasted with Q4’s framing that the "spring selling season will be a key driver of our financial results this year" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski, Q4 2025).

Financial Results

  • "Home sale revenues in the first quarter were $3.3 billion compared with $3.7 billion last year" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), and "PulteGroup's net income for the first quarter was $347 million or $1.79 per share" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski).
  • "On a year-over-year basis, the first quarter net new orders increased 3% to 8,034 homes with a value of $4.6 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), while "absorption paces decreased by 5% to 2.6 homes per month" and "our cancellation rate as a percentage of starting backlog in the quarter was 13% compared with 11% last year" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski).
  • "Our results benefited from 18% order growth in Florida" (President, CEO & Director Marshall), and "orders increased in every Florida market and were up 18% statewide" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski).
  • "For the first quarter, we reported gross margin of 24.4%, which is down from 27.5% in the first quarter of 2025" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), and "within our Q1 home sale cost of revenues is approximately $6 million or 20 basis points associated with land impairments" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski).
  • "Q1 house costs were down 5% from the first quarter of last year to $75 per square foot" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), and "Pulte's Financial Services operations reported first quarter pretax income of $13 million which is down from pretax income of $36 million in the first quarter of 2025" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski).

Q&A

  • John Lovallo, UBS: Asked about the bridge to full-year gross margin and whether "10.9% carries throughout the year"; President, CEO & Director Marshall answered, "we are assuming a higher incentive load, but we'd expect it to likely come down" due to mix and clearing specs, and said, "Q2 is going to be a low point" because "a bunch more [specs] are closing in Q2." Lovallo also asked about a stronger spring if "interest rates came back towards 6%"; President, CEO & Director Marshall said, "there's a bias to the upside" if rates fall and disruptions ease.
  • Alan Ratner, Zelman: Asked about incentive differences by price point and "BTO versus spec"; President, CEO & Director Marshall said, "there's definitely more incentive on spec broadly" and "more incentive as a percentage on first-time spec." Ratner asked to quantify land banking; Executive VP & CFO Ossowski said, "we have about 18,000 with land bankers so it's about 8% of the book," and added "usually about a 15% deposit" with "rates...in the low double-digit range."
  • Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI: Asked why 2026 cash flow is guided near $1B; Executive VP & CFO Ossowski said the driver is house inventory: "we'll see a little bit of an incremental increase" tied to more build-to-order. Kim asked when the 60/40 build-to-order mix could be reached; President, CEO & Director Marshall said, "I think it might take a tad longer than the end of this year, but not much beyond Q1 of next year."
  • Anthony Pettinari, Citi: Asked about lumber/commodity lags and Middle East impacts; Executive VP & CFO Ossowski said lumber is "usually two quarters out" and reaffirmed "house costs would be flat to slightly down," while President, CEO & Director Marshall said other inputs would be "later in the year" and the team wants "patience" and not "overreact." Pettinari asked if incentives changed through the quarter; Executive VP & CFO Ossowski said, "They're fairly steady across the quarter."
  • Michael Rehaut, JPMorgan: Asked if incentive stability referred to closings or orders; President, CEO & Director Marshall responded, "I think you made things up there" and emphasized incentives are reported "on closings" and Q2 is impacted by Q1 spec sales closing. Rehaut asked about Florida/inventory; President, CEO & Director Marshall said Florida is "at the best point that we've seen it in a while," and Executive VP & CFO Ossowski added, "We have seen inventory come down in certain locations."
  • Michael Dahl, RBC: Asked about second-half mix visibility for margin; Executive VP & CFO Ossowski said it is based on "what we're seeing on the sales floor today" and that "the build-to-order mix and the active adult are the 2 biggest components." Dahl asked about finished spec targets and starts; President, CEO & Director Marshall said the company is "inside the target range" and that "we're not going to chase the volume" and noted "cycle times...less than 100 days."
  • Richard Reid, Wells Fargo: Asked if ASP declines imply move-up/active-adult price cuts; President, CEO & Director Marshall said, "There are some communities where we have taken price cuts" and linked impairments to "typically...the price cuts," adding "it's just two communities." Reid asked about lower Financial Services gains and ARMs; Executive VP & CFO Ossowski said ARMs were "9% of all closings in the first quarter versus 7% for all of last year" and cited rate-lock timing and investments in "people and technology."
  • Matthew Bouley, Barclays: Asked about the implied step-up in second-half margins and build-to-order closings mix; Executive VP & CFO Ossowski cited "richer mix of build-to-order" plus less finished-spec impact, and President, CEO & Director Marshall said, "it's not as if we've got a gigantic chasm to cross" to stay within "24.5% to 25%." Bouley asked about land-price easing timing; President, CEO & Director Marshall said the benefit typically hits in "18- to 24-month" timing and lot cost split is a "general rule of thumb...50-50."
  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe: Asked about accelerating buybacks with near-zero leverage; President, CEO & Director Marshall said "we don't think it's in the best interest long term...to do a levered buyback" and that "a debt-to-cap ratio will be an outcome as opposed to a targeted goal." Allinson asked about Midwest softness; President, CEO & Director Marshall said, "we're still really happy with our Midwest performance" and attributed variability partly to "a real winter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative (analysts): Several questions pressed on incentives, margin durability, cash flow conversion, and land banking economics, including a sharper exchange where President, CEO & Director Marshall told an analyst, "I think you made things up there."
  • Slightly positive (management): Management repeatedly used reaffirmation language and emphasized stability, including "we reaffirm our previous guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski) and "we remain optimistic about long-term housing demand" (President, CEO & Director Marshall), while acknowledging uncertainty: "there is uncertainty about how events will develop" (President, CEO & Director Marshall).
  • Tone vs. prior quarter: Q4 emphasized optimism into spring and strategic moves (including a divestiture decision), while Q1 paired "reaffirm" guidance with more pointed commentary on geopolitical disruption and affordability pressure, including "we didn't have any of the current geopolitical disruption on our bingo card" (President, CEO & Director Marshall).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance posture shifted from setting the full-year frame to reiterating it: Q4 set 2026 closings, ASP, and margin ranges; Q1 stressed, "This keeps us on track with our previous guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski) and maintained full-year ranges while calling Q2 the "low point" for margins.
  • Incentive load moved higher: Q4 cited incentives of "9.9% of gross sales price" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski, Q4 2025) versus Q1 at "10.9% of gross sales price" (President, CEO & Director Marshall).
  • Spec-clearing progress accelerated: Q1 reported "1.4 finished specs per community" (President, CEO & Director Marshall) and a reduction of finished specs by "nearly 500 homes or 24%" in 90 days (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), aligning with Q4’s stated intent to rebalance back toward 60% build-to-order.
  • Capital return capacity increased: Q1 announced an "additional $1.5 billion" authorization, bringing "total availability to $2.1 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), versus Q4’s "$983 million remaining" authorization (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski, Q4 2025).

Risks and Concerns

  • "Incentives...reached 10.9% of gross sales price" (President, CEO & Director Marshall), and management said it comes "at a price" even as the company balances inventory turnover and margins.
  • "Impairments were triggered in two communities" (Executive VP & CFO Ossowski), which he tied to "today's competitive market dynamics" and "efforts to clear excess spec inventory."
  • Regional softness remained a focus: "Texas and West markets...remained slower" (President, CEO & Director Marshall) and "we still have work to do on clearing some final spec inventory in California and Washington" (President, CEO & Director Marshall).

Final Takeaway

PulteGroup management described Q1 as "increasingly more complicated" while reporting $3.3B in home sale revenues and reaffirming full-year 2026 closings of 28,500-29,000 alongside a 24.5%-25.0% gross margin outlook that management said is likely "towards the lower end." Executives repeatedly pointed to elevated incentives and a deliberate shift back toward build-to-order as key drivers of near-term margin and cash flow timing, while highlighting Florida outperformance, a reduced finished spec position inside the targeted 1 to 1.5 per community range, and a new $1.5B share repurchase authorization that lifts total capacity to $2.1B.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Union Pacific affirms 2026 mid-single-digit EPS growth while targeting STB refiling on April 30
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Union Pacific (UNP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “2026 started strong as we delivered record first quarter results,” and Union Pacific “reported first quarter records in operating income and net income,” said CEO Vincenzo Vena.

  • Vena highlighted Q1 profitability and efficiency, saying the company “reported net income of $1.7 billion,” “earnings per share of $2.87,” and “our operating ratio improved 80 basis points to 59.9%” excluding merger costs.

  • “Operating revenue of $6.2 billion increased 3% versus last year,” while “freight revenue of $5.9 billion grew 4% on 1% lower volume,” said CFO Jennifer Hamann.

  • Hamann said costs rose with inflation and merger activity, but were partially offset by productivity: “total operating expense increased 3% to $3.8 billion,” while “record first quarter workforce productivity” enabled “a 5% smaller workforce.”

  • Marketing and segment demand was mixed, led by bulk and industrial strength, while premium remained pressured. “Bulk segment… was up 10%… driven by a 12% increase in volume,” including coal strength and “record volume” in grain, said EVP Kenny Rocker; meanwhile “Premium revenue… declined 5% on a 9% decrease in volume,” with “international intermodal volumes… down 28% versus last year.”

  • Operational execution was positioned as a key differentiator. EVP Eric Gehringer said Union Pacific “set first quarter records in all 6 of our key performance and efficiency metrics,” including “freight car velocity increased 9% to 235 miles per day” and “best-ever terminal dwell of 19.7 hours.”

  • On the Norfolk Southern merger process, Vena said, “We are 100% on track with filing a revised application on April 30,” adding, “We are confident the additional information we are providing meets the STB’s expectations.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated full-year guidance. Hamann said, “we are affirming our 2026 outlook,” including “reported earnings per share of mid-single-digit growth and operating ratio improvement.”

  • Fuel was framed as a near-term margin headwind. Hamann said the original 2026 diesel assumption “is now much harder to predict,” and in April the company is “paying a little north of $4 a gallon,” which she said “will certainly pressure margins, particularly here in the second quarter.”

  • Longer-term targets were reiterated. Hamann said, “Beyond 2026, we remain committed to attaining our 3-year CAGR target of high single-digit to low double-digit EPS growth throughout 2027,” and Vena similarly said the company is “affirming our long-term guidance… through 2027.”

  • Relative to last quarter’s framing, Q1 commentary put more emphasis on near-term fuel volatility (and its second-quarter effect) while keeping full-year EPS growth and operating ratio improvement language intact.

Financial Results

  • Union Pacific reported “net income of $1.7 billion… earnings per share of $2.87,” and “adjusted… EPS of $2.93,” said Vena.

  • Hamann reported Q1 revenue and key drivers: “operating revenue of $6.2 billion increased 3%,” “fuel surcharge revenue of $608 million increased $43 million,” and “other revenue declined 4% to $324 million.”

  • Cash generation and balance sheet changes were highlighted as investment-relevant. Hamann said “cash from operations totaled $2.4 billion,” “free cash flow… $630 million,” and “net debt decreased $1.2 billion as we repaid our long-term debt,” ending with “adjusted debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 2.5x.”

  • Cost items management called out included “fuel expense grew 7%… from $2.51 to $2.69 per gallon,” and “purchased services & materials… increased 7% as a result of merger-related costs,” said Hamann.

Q&A

  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: asked whether the extended merger process reduces confidence; CEO Vena replied, “We are more convicted now than we ever have been,” and said he is “much more convicted” the merger is “good for the country and good for Union Pacific.”

  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: pressed on operating ratio improvement with fuel as a headwind; CFO Hamann said fuel will “pressure margins… here in the second quarter,” but added, “we are still confident for the full year that we will be able to improve our operating ratio.”

  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore ISI: asked about spare capacity if macro improves; EVP Gehringer said “we have latent capacity,” citing train length gains and “between $500 million and $700 million a year in capacity projects,” while Vena said the railroad is running with “over 100 locomotives on the mainline less.”

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA: questioned merger concessions and why guidance language shifted to “reported”; CFO Hamann said, “we… wanted to be clear… when we talk about the EPS growth, that’s off of our reported,” citing “merger costs… and… we’re not buying back shares right now,” while Vena said, “it’s pretty hard to come up with concessions that make sense.”

  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays: raised market-share concerns; Vena said the combined share is “38% or 39%… of GTMs,” and argued rail has “low double-digit capture of the true market.”

  • David Vernon, Bernstein: asked about “committed gateway pricing” and customer feedback; EVP Rocker said “520 customers… 700 commercial partners… 2,000 in total… have signed a letter of support.”

  • Richa Talwar, Deutsche Bank: probed headcount productivity sustainability and transferability; EVP Gehringer said “there is no finish line,” and “they absolutely are transferable.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to pressing around the merger timeline, concessions, and regulatory scrutiny, reflected in questions such as “we’re about to restart the clock” (Scott Group, Wolfe Research) and concerns about market share (Brandon Oglenski, Barclays).

  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks (record metrics and affirmed outlook) and assertive in Q&A on the merger, including Vena’s “we are more convicted now than we ever have been,” while acknowledging pressures like fuel (“will certainly pressure margins,” Hamann).

  • Compared with Q4 2025, merger-related questions remained dominant, but Q1 leaned more into conviction and process specifics (“filing… on April 30,” Vena), while shifting some Q&A attention toward fuel volatility and capacity to absorb growth.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Merger process detail changed from Q4’s “refile… as soon as possible” and “targeting closing in the first half of 2027” to Q1’s specific action date: “filing a revised application on April 30,” said Vena.

  • Q1 introduced a clearer near-term fuel framing versus Q4’s broader inflation backdrop: Hamann said April fuel is “a little north of $4 a gallon,” which will be a “headwind… particularly here in the second quarter.”

  • Business narrative shifted at the margin: Q1 emphasized bulk strength (coal and grain) and domestic intermodal momentum (“third consecutive record quarter,” Rocker), while Q4 emphasized softer macro expectations and discussed weather disruptions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Fuel-price volatility was the most explicit near-term risk discussed. Hamann said the diesel estimate “is now much harder to predict,” and Q2 margins will be pressured given fuel “a little north of $4 a gallon.”

  • Premium demand softness remained a key volume risk. Rocker said “international intermodal volumes… declined 28%,” and management said “international intermodal volumes will remain subdued.”

  • Regulatory risk and timing uncertainty around the merger remained central, though management emphasized confidence. Vena said, “We are 100% on track with filing a revised application on April 30,” and later said, “We are more convicted today than I was when we put the application in the first time.”

Final Takeaway

Union Pacific management characterized Q1 2026 as a record quarter on profitability and operating performance, while reaffirming its 2026 outlook for mid-single-digit reported EPS growth and operating ratio improvement despite a stated Q2 fuel headwind tied to April diesel pricing “a little north of $4 a gallon.” Executives repeatedly positioned service, productivity, and capacity as enabling continued share gains (notably in domestic intermodal) and emphasized merger progress and confidence, with CEO Vincenzo Vena stating the company is “100% on track with filing a revised application on April 30” and “more convicted now than we ever have been” about ultimate approval and benefits.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: West Fraser Timber cut at Raymond James as earnings inflection emerges but seasonality looms
data.description:
West Fraser Timber (WFG) up 1.3% in Thursday's trading as Raymond James downgraded shares to Market Perform from Outperform with a $75 price target, down from $85 previously, reflecting an updated commodity outlook and cautious near-term view on fundamentals.

Raymond James analyst Daryl Swetlishoff said West Fraser's (WFG) near-term fundamentals remain supportive into Q2, backstopped by firm order files and strong QTD lumber price gains, setting up for a potential step-change in earnings and positive free cash flow inflection.

Despite a more constructive near-term earnings outlook, Swetlishoff said seasonal softening in building materials pricing into the summer could weigh on sentiment.

At the same time, the analyst reiterated Strong Buy ratings on Adentra (HDIUF) and Doman Building Materials (CWXZF) on the back of seasonal tailwinds, improving volumes, and a favorable M&A backdrop supported by strong balance sheets and available dry powder, setting up relative outperformance into Q2-Q3.

data.title: White House reviewing proposed rules by SEC to encourage IPOs
data.description:
The White House is reviewing proposed rules related to making IPOs and capital raises easier by cutting disclosure requirements or accelerating the registration process.

Paul Atkins, chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, delivered keynote remarks at The Economic Club of Washington on April 21. He said that as the disclosure burden expanded, the number of our public companies diminished.

To reverse the precipitous decline in public companies, he disclosed near-term proposals that included adopting a regulatory IPO "on-ramp" that supplements the concept that Congress designed in the JOBS Act; expanding the existing accommodations that are currently available only for emerging and smaller companies to more businesses; providing nearly all public companies with an easier path to "shelf registration"; and giving companies the optionality for a quarterly or semiannual regulatory filing cadence.

The SEC is set to hold a meeting on Tuesday, April 28, to explore ways to encourage more companies to go public.

The proposed rule changes were received by the White House on Wednesday.

The White House will send it back to the SEC with any edits for the agency to vote on, and then it will be made available to the public for feedback. The process typically takes about one and a half to two years.

data.title: Graco maintains 2026 outlook for low single-digit organic growth while planning $90M-$100M capex
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Graco Inc. (GGG) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Yesterday, Graco reported first quarter sales of $540 million, up 2% from the same quarter last year.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Christopher Knutson)

  • “Organic revenue started the year slower than expected, particularly in January, but business activity improved steadily as the quarter progressed with bookings up 3% at actual currency rates, driving nearly a $26 million increase in backlog, primarily in our Industrial segment.” (President, CEO & Director Mark Sheahan)

  • “Tariffs increased product costs by $7 million in the quarter.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

  • “The Middle East region represents about $35 million of sales on a full year basis for Graco. To date, we've not seen any significant impact on demand or operations, though the environment remains uncertain.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan)

  • “Finally, I'd like to take a moment to welcome Sanjiv Gupta to Graco.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan) “In addition, I want to recognize and thank David Lowe for his more than 30 years of dedicated service as he prepares for retirement.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan)

Outlook

  • “As a result, we're maintaining our 2026 revenue guidance of low single-digit organic growth on a constant currency basis and mid-single-digit growth, including contributions from acquisitions.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan)

  • “Looking ahead, second half comparisons are more favorable, reflecting an easier Contractor comparison in the third quarter and the expected timing of project activity in the Industrial businesses towards the end of the year.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan)

  • “Based on current exchange rates and assuming similar volume, product mix and business mix as in 2025, currency is expected to have a 1% favorable impact on net sales and a 2% favorable impact on net earnings for the full year 2026.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

Financial results

  • “Reported net earnings were $119 million, down 5% or $0.70 per diluted share.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson) “Excluding excess tax benefits from stock option exercises, adjusted non-GAAP net earnings were $0.66 per diluted share, down 6%.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

  • “Gross margin decreased 60 basis points versus the first quarter last year.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson) “Operating earnings as a percentage of sales were 26% compared to 27% in the same period last year.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

  • “Cash provided by operations totaled $120 million for the year, down $5 million or 4%.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson) “Year-to-date uses of cash include share repurchases of 189,000, shares totaling $16 million, dividends of $49 million and capital expenditures of $12 million.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

Q&A

  • Deane Dray, RBC Capital Markets: “maybe you can just kind of take us through the major verticals and kind of what surprised you versus expectations?” Title Sheahan: “our Industrial bookings in the quarter were actually up mid-single digits, which was good. And unfortunately, we weren't able to convert that into revenue that you all saw.”

  • Deane Dray, RBC Capital Markets: “Can you talk about pricing?” Title Sheahan: “I really have no concerns on the gross margin line for the rest of the year.” Executive Officer Lowe: “we have covered tariff costs… we have been pursuing around the world our annual pricing adjustment drumbeat.”

  • Mitchell Moore, KeyBanc Capital Markets: “what's giving you confidence in the outlook?” Title Sheahan: “we're up low single digit on our bookings for the first quarter.” VP Knutson: “we've also seen another $21 million build in the backlog.”

  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: “how much of the total build has been your Gema business?” Title Sheahan: “I didn't see anything jump off the page at me that says that they're heavily weighted towards the powder business.”

  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson: “sell-in, sell-through trends… home center and pro paint channel?” Title Sheahan: “In terms of sell-in, sell-through, there's not a big difference.” Executive Officer Lowe: “the foot traffic has not deteriorated year-over-year.”

  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson: “comment on how you're thinking about the M&A outlook” Title Sheahan: “Our pipelines are well populated.”

  • Joseph Ritchie, Goldman Sachs: “any progress on that specifically” (licensing revenue) Title Sheahan: “we didn't really book anything here in Q1.”

  • Andrew Buscaglia, BNP Paribas: “is there something more structural?” Title Sheahan: “There's nothing structurally wrong with the company. It's still extremely profitable. It still generates a tremendous amount of cash.”

  • Walter Liptak, Seaport Research: “How are you thinking about buybacks versus M&A deals?” CFO & Treasurer Sanjiv Gupta: “We'll be very disciplined with our capital allocation framework… any excess cash will be returned to the shareholders, and we'll be doing it very opportunistically as we've always done.”

Sentiment analysis

  • “slightly negative” analyst tone, centered on durability of growth and macro/tariffs, including “it just seems like the top line can't grow.” (Andrew Buscaglia, BNP Paribas)

  • “slightly positive” management tone, leaning on bookings/backlog and maintained guide, including “we're encouraged by demand trends” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan) and “we have confidence we'll get there by the end of the year.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

  • Compared with last quarter, management again emphasized caution but stayed firm on the full-year frame, echoing prior conservatism such as “pretty cautious at this point” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan, Q4 2025) while Q1 2026 added more discussion of conversion timing and tariffs assessment.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 shifted from Q4 2025’s organic growth to organic contraction, with Q1 noting “a 6% decline in organic sales” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson) versus Q4 reporting “organic sales another 2%” contribution (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson, Q4 2025).

  • The licensing topic faded from a recognized benefit in Q4 to no contribution in Q1, moving from “upfront electric motor license fee revenue of $5 million in the quarter” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson, Q4 2025) to “we didn't really book anything here in Q1.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan)

  • Tariff cost impact increased in Q1 commentary, moving from “Tariffs affected product costs by $4 million in the quarter” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson, Q4 2025) to “Tariffs increased product costs by $7 million in the quarter.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

  • Leadership messaging changed materially, with Q1 introducing “welcome Sanjiv Gupta to Graco” and noting retirement preparation for “David Lowe” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan), following Q4 where Lowe was still CFO & Treasurer.

Risks and concerns

  • “construction demand remains softer than we would like, particularly in the Americas” and “we expect those conditions to persist this year.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan)

  • “the environment remains uncertain” in the Middle East, with management monitoring “order patterns and logistics carefully.” (President, CEO & Director Sheahan)

  • “We're still working on assessing how much that's going to impact us” regarding Section 232 tariff changes to “full component.” (VP, Controller & Chief Accounting Officer Knutson)

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as a slower-than-expected start with organic sales down, but emphasized improving activity through the quarter, Industrial and semiconductor booking momentum, and a growing backlog that management said supports maintaining 2026 guidance for low single-digit organic growth (constant currency) and mid-single-digit growth including acquisitions. Leadership transition (Gupta joining as CFO and Lowe preparing to retire) and tariff-related cost pressure and assessment were highlighted alongside continued discipline on capital allocation, including dividends, buybacks, and an active M&A pipeline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Honeywell forecasts 2% to 4% Q2 organic sales growth as Aerospace spin is set for June 29
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Honeywell (HON) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Honeywell delivered strong results in the first quarter, building on the momentum from 2025 despite a complex geopolitical backdrop and temporary mechanical supply chain constraints in Aerospace." (Chairman & CEO Vimal Kapur) "Orders grew 7% organically" and "we drove backlog to over $38 billion with book-to-bill above 1.1," while "we expanded margin 90 basis points to over 23%."
  • "We announced the sale of our Productivity Solutions and Services and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions businesses... which we expect to close in the second half of 2026." (Chairman & CEO Kapur) "We are also excited to announce that now we expect to complete the Honeywell Aerospace spin-off in the third quarter on June 29," and he said Honeywell "successfully raised $20 billion of Aerospace spin financing" with investment-grade ratings.
  • "In quarter 1, the Middle East conflict drove a roughly 0.5% impact to revenue for all of Honeywell." (Chairman & CEO Kapur) He said the company "secured over $2 billion in project wins over the past 3 quarters" in Process, and described an LNG push including an agreement "to provide integrated liquefied natural gas pretreatment and liquefaction solution for Commonwealth LNG" and NextDecade’s Rio Grande project.
  • "Adjusted earnings per share of $2.45 was up 11%." (Senior VP & CFO Mike Stepniak) "We ended the quarter with nearly $100 million of free cash flow," and "we returned $1.8 billion to shareholders" including "roughly $1 billion of share repurchases" and "$800 million of dividends."

Outlook

  • "We anticipate second quarter organic sales growth of 2% to 4%." (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak) He added, "Process Automation and Technology will be slightly weaker than first quarter due to incremental pressure stemming from the Middle East conflict."
  • "We expect segment margin to be in the range of 22.2% to 22.5%." (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak) "Adjusted EPS is expected to be $2.40 at the midpoint," and he said, "On a normalized tax basis, EPS in the second quarter would be roughly $2.55 at the midpoint."
  • "We're maintaining our organic growth outlook of 3% to 6%." (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak) "While we outperformed our expectations in the first quarter, the ongoing geopolitical situation warrants prudence," and "we are, therefore, maintaining our full year segment margin guidance of 22.7% to 23.1%."

Financial results

  • "Sales grew 2% organically." (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak) He said total company "segment margin expanded 90 basis points to 23.3%" and "orders grew 7% organically" with "book-to-bill above 1.1 and 15% increase in backlog."
  • "Building Automation surpassed our expectation once again... with sales up 8% organically." (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak) He also reported "Aerospace sales grew 3% organically" and said Process Automation and Technology "sales were down 6% organically" due to timing delays and Middle East impacts.
  • "We ended the quarter with nearly $100 million of free cash flow, down from $200 million last year." (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak) He attributed pressure to "timing of collections in the Middle East and inventory headwinds in Aerospace," adding, "Collections have improved meaningfully in April."

Q&A

  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe Research: asked for more detail on Aerospace supply chain; President & CEO Currier: "it was a very acute transitory issue, specifically with some key suppliers in the mechanical space" and "we started to see that recovery in the March time frame... carry itself over into April."
  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe Research: asked why Q2 margins step down; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak: "we have... a mix pressure from catalyst sales or lack of catalyst sales in the second quarter," while Chairman & CEO Kapur added, "the loss of revenue we're having in Middle East is also high-margin revenue."
  • Julian Mitchell, Barclays: asked about PA&T ramp; Chairman & CEO Kapur: "we lost revenue... about 0.5%... in Q1 and about 1% in Q2" and said the second half ramp is supported by backlog, with "high single" growth for PA&T in the second half.
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: pressed on aftermarket exposure and margins; President & CEO Currier: "our growth is limited by supply and not about the demand" and said Q1 margin benefited because "our mix was much more favorable electrical versus mechanical."
  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research: asked about tax leakage and Quantinuum; Senior VP & CFO Stepniak: "there won't be any tax leakage related to these 2 transactions," while Investor Relations Macaluso said on Quantinuum, "we're subject to rules restricting our ability to share really any further details."
  • Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley: asked if Aero issue was destocking; President & CEO Currier: "It was absolutely not any destocking" and "purely supplier-centric."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative, with repeated probing on Aerospace supply chain, quarterly margin step-down, and Middle East risk, including "I'm sorry to beat a dead horse on this Aerospace issue" (Nicole DeBlase, Deutsche Bank).
  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks ("delivered strong results") but more defensive/clarifying in Q&A, including "It was absolutely not any destocking" (President & CEO Currier) and "we are taking a prudent approach to our guidance" (Chairman & CEO Kapur).
  • Versus last quarter: the Q1 call carried more event-driven risk discussion (Middle East disruption and supplier constraints), while Q4 emphasized transformation progress and 2026 setup; both periods reiterated the Aerospace spin timeline, but Q1 provided a firm date ("June 29").

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Portfolio and separation: Q4 framed the plan as "we now expect to complete the Aerospace spin in the third quarter of 2026" (Chairman & CEO Kapur), while Q1 tightened the timeline: "now expected to complete... on June 29" (Chairman & CEO Kapur) and disclosed "$20 billion of Aerospace spin financing" (Chairman & CEO Kapur).
  • Demand and operating narrative: Q4 highlighted "orders up 23%" and backlog "over $37 billion" (Chairman & CEO Kapur), while Q1 highlighted "Orders grew 7% organically" and backlog "over $38 billion" (Chairman & CEO Kapur) alongside disruptions from "temporary mechanical supply chain constraints" and the Middle East conflict.
  • Q&A emphasis: Q4 questions leaned into Quantinuum investment, pricing durability, and stranded costs; Q1 questions concentrated on Aerospace supply constraints, catalyst/mix-driven margin step-down, and the Middle East revenue impact and rebuild cadence.

Risks and concerns

  • "We are taking a prudent approach to our guidance given the uncertainty surrounding the conflict in Middle East." (Chairman & CEO Kapur) He said guidance assumed the conflict "persists through the end of the quarter" with "logistics and shipment delays".
  • "Certain critical suppliers experienced temporary constraints to start the year." (Chairman & CEO Kapur) President & CEO Currier characterized the issue as "very acute" and tied it to "key suppliers in the mechanical space" affecting Engines, Power Systems, and Control Systems.
  • "We expect to de-consolidate the results of Quantinuum in the second quarter" but "we are not adjusting our segment margin or adjusted EPS guidance at this time." (Senior VP & CFO Stepniak)

Final takeaway

Management described a quarter defined by strong order/backlog execution and margin expansion alongside two near-term disruptions: a Middle East-driven revenue/logistics impact and an Aerospace mechanical supplier constraint that the company said improved in March and carried into April. Leadership set a firm Aerospace spin date of June 29, reiterated full-year organic growth of 3% to 6% and segment margin of 22.7% to 23.1%, and guided Q2 to 2% to 4% organic sales growth and $2.40 adjusted EPS at the midpoint, while calling out Q2 margin pressure from catalyst mix and high-margin Middle East disruptions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Churchill Downs rallies after earnings, preps for Derby Week
data.description:
Churchill Downs (CHDN) announced on Thursday that the 152nd Kentucky Derby will feature a larger slate of onsite brand activations designed to make the fan experience more interactive across beauty, food and beverage, and technology via both returning signature partners and new collaborations.

The Derby promotions will feature L’Oréal Paris as the first beauty partner, as well as fresh activations from brands such as Graeter’s, Hallmark, Mitsubishi Electric, and others. The company described the effort as part of a broader push to create memorable touchpoints from gate entry through the final race while keeping the event rooted in Derby tradition.

Last year, the Kentucky Derby saw on‑track attendance at Churchill Downs of 147,406, a step down from the prior year but still among the largest live sports crowds in the U.S. All-sources wagering handle on the 2025 Derby itself reached roughly $335M, with the Derby–Oaks combined handle approaching about $410M, both up modestly year over year and continuing the event’s long-term growth trajectory in betting activity and monetization. 

Shares of Churchill Downs (CHDN) rallied 8.2% in late action on Thursday after the company beat Q1 revenue, adjusted EPS, and adjusted EBITDA estimates.

data.title: NextEra Energy soars to all-time high as Q1 profit more than doubled
data.description:
NextEra Energy (NEE) up 6.8% in Thursday's trading to an all-time intraday high of $96.70 after reporting Q1 adjusted earnings that topped Wall Street consensus estimates and said it expects to finalize agreements in the next three months for giant gas-fired power projects that will be backed by Japan and used for data centers in the ​U.S.

The U.S. and Japan have promised billions of dollars in investments to build new power plant projects ​for data centers, and NextEra (NEE) is acting as the developer and operator on two projects—one in Pennsylvania and one in Texas—that are expected to total nearly 10 GW.

CEO John Ketchum said NextEra (NEE) is working on finalizing the deal terms with Japan to move forward with its plans, although he did not reveal the names of specific customers ​for the projects.

Q1 profit surged to $2.18B, or $1.04/share, from a profit of $833M, or $0.40/share, in the year-earlier quarter, while revenues rose to $6.7B, up sharply from $6.25B a year ago but below the $7.27B analyst consensus estimate.

NextEra's (NEE) regulated utility, Florida Power & Light, posted Q1 net income of $1.46B, or $0.70/share, up 11.1% Y/Y, lifted by an increase in customers and continued capital investments.

FPL added nearly 100K customers during the quarter and expanded its total owned and operated ​solar capacity to more than ​8.5 GW.

The ​company's renewables and ⁠storage unit, NextEra Energy Resources (NEE), contributed $1.02B, or $0.49/share, and added 4 GW of projects to its backlog, including 1.3 GW of battery storage, raising the total to ~33 GW.

NextEra (NEE) also reaffirmed guidance for full-year adjusted earnings of $3.92-$4.02/share and is targeting the higher end of the range, as well as its target of a compound annual growth rate of at least 8% through 2032.

data.title: PG&E reaffirms 2026 core EPS $1.64-$1.66 and 9%+ growth through 2030 while planning 0 new equity through 2030
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PG&E Corporation (PCG) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Today, we announced core earnings per share for the first quarter of $0.43… and reaffirm our full year 2026 core EPS guidance of $1.64 to $1.66.” (CEO & Director Patricia Poppe)

  • “Looking forward, we're reaffirming our EPS growth guidance for 2027 through 2030, which is unchanged at 9% plus annually… including 0 new equity issuance needs through 2030.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • “In February, our Diablo Canyon nuclear power plant received the final state permit approvals needed to support extended operations through 2030. And in early April, the Nuclear Regulatory Commission granted Diablo Canyon a 20-year license extension… although further action by the state is required in order to operate beyond 2030.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • “Projects are moving through our development pipeline with our final engineering stage increasing to 4.6 gigawatts… In total, customer interest exceeded an additional 10 gigawatts… There's no single project driving these totals… We're committed to only adding load that is definitively rate reducing. We simply need to get the pricing right.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • “Core earnings for the quarter were $0.43… Customer capital investments contributed $0.06… Nonfuel O&M savings contributed an additional $0.02… Timing and other was a $0.03 tailwind.” (CFO & Executive VP Carolyn Burke)

Outlook

  • “This strong start puts us solidly on track to deliver again and reaffirm our full year 2026 core EPS guidance of $1.64 to $1.66.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • “We're reaffirming our EPS growth guidance for 2027 through 2030, which is unchanged at 9% plus annually.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • Management’s guidance posture shifted from Q4’s “raising and tightening our 2026 core EPS guidance range” to Q1’s reaffirmation of the same $1.64 to $1.66 range. (CEO & Director Poppe)

Financial Results

  • $0.43 core EPS vs. $0.39 (analysts’ Q1 2026 EPS estimate). (CEO & Director Poppe; analystsEstimates)

  • “Of that, $0.02 reflects ongoing execution of our capital plan… We also have a $0.04 benefit related to February's final commission decision in our 2023… application.” (CFO & Executive VP Burke)

  • “There is no change to our 5-year $73 billion capital plan through 2030… and we still see at least $5 billion of incremental customer investment opportunity outside the current plan.” (CFO & Executive VP Burke)

  • “In February, we took advantage of favorable market conditions… issued $1 billion of parent level junior subordinated notes… At the utility, we issued $2.2 billion of first mortgage bonds.” (CFO & Executive VP Burke)

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: asked how to think about capital allocation (including buybacks) if SB 254 Phase 2 is only partial; CEO & Director Poppe responded, “there's obviously minimum outcome to prevent additional costs being borne by shareholders,” adding that if the “minimum outcome” is not achieved, “all aspects of the plan will have to be on the table.”

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: asked when data center load becomes material to earnings and drives transmission investment; CEO & Director Poppe said the company aims to “make the plan better… by pulling in that transmission and data center load growth, if it makes it more affordable for customers.”

  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: asked what SB 254 outcome is “sufficient” and how to think about shareholder contributions; CEO & Director Poppe said, “our minimum is very important that we have an ability to see and model and quantify what that tail risk is,” and added, “if it doesn't improve the status quo, then contributions would be unacceptable.”

  • Steven Fleishman, Wolfe: asked which wildfire report structures best match PG&E’s needs; CEO & Director Poppe highlighted “the whole of society look,” including “hardening our communities,” “liability limits and liability reform,” and “any kind of state backstop.”

  • David Arcaro, Morgan Stanley: asked timing and pace of data center demand and when pipeline refills; CEO & Director Poppe said Cluster ’26 showed “10-plus gigawatts showing interest,” noted engineering over “the next 6 to 8 months,” and reiterated, “We still expect to have about 1.8 gigawatts online by 2030.”

  • Anthony Crowdell, Mizuho: asked conversion from final engineering to construction and whether the $5 billion incremental opportunity depends on undergrounding approvals; CEO & Director Poppe said conversion should be “high,” but “we've not been at this stage with this volume before,” and answered on the $5 billion: “Unrelated.”

  • Gregg Orrill, UBS: asked about rate case settlement likelihood; CEO & Director Poppe said, “we would never rule out settlement,” while also citing “pretty strong indications from the CPUC that they like to do a fully adjudicated GRC.”

  • Ryan Levine, Citi: pressed on weather/wildfire season and capital allocation credit metrics and then buybacks/dividends; CEO & Director Poppe said PG&E’s strategy is “not to count on weather prediction, we count on being ready every day,” while CFO & Executive VP Burke said “everything is on the table” post-SB 254, and CEO & Director Poppe added, “we're just not going to rack and stack the alternatives here today.”

  • Beatriz Bomfim de Abreu, Goldman Sachs: asked on CAISO transmission approvals and financing, plus Diablo Canyon beyond 2030; CEO & Director Poppe said CAISO “awarded 25 projects… totaling $4.16 billion of projects for PG&E,” and on Diablo Canyon, “it is up to the legislature on whether the plant would be extended beyond 2030.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on urgency and sufficiency of wildfire liability reform and capital allocation contingencies, including questions on “buybacks,” what is “sufficient,” and how “draconian” affordability proposals could affect returns. (Wells Fargo; Barclays)

  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing execution and reaffirmed targets, while turning more conditional in Q&A around SB 254 outcomes: “if we don't get a minimum outcome… we'll have to look at… our entire capital allocation plan.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • Versus Q4, management language was less about “raising and tightening” guidance and more about reaffirmation and legislative dependency; analysts remained focused on SB 254 timing and the decision tree for capital allocation if reform stalls. (CEO & Director Poppe)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from Q4’s “raising and tightening our 2026 core EPS guidance range… to $1.64 to $1.66” to Q1’s reaffirmation of the same $1.64 to $1.66 range. (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • Load pipeline commentary advanced from “almost 3.6 gigawatts” in final engineering to “4.6 gigawatts,” with continued emphasis that pricing must be “rate reducing.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • New Q1 operational and regulatory milestones included Diablo Canyon’s “20-year license extension” and CAISO awards totaling “$4.16 billion,” while Q4 highlighted the launch of Emberpoint with Lockheed Martin. (CEO & Director Poppe)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Our minimum is very important that we have an ability to see and model and quantify what that tail risk is.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

  • “If we stop seeing progress towards reforming the wildfire risk model, you can be sure that we will actively reevaluate all aspects of our capital allocation plan.” (CFO & Executive VP Burke)

  • Management described mitigation levers as “PSPS, EPSS and continuous monitoring,” alongside undergrounding plans that are intended to “combine safety and improved reliability and lower maintenance costs.” (CEO & Director Poppe)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a reaffirmation quarter: core EPS of $0.43 supported reaffirmed 2026 core EPS guidance of $1.64 to $1.66 and unchanged 9%+ annual EPS growth guidance for 2027-2030, alongside an unchanged $73B capital plan and financing plan that assumes no new common equity through 2030. The most consequential swing factor in management’s messaging remained SB 254 Phase 2, with repeated emphasis that an acceptable outcome must make wildfire “tail risk” quantifiable—while warning that if the “minimum outcome” is not achieved, “all aspects” of capital allocation could be revisited. Meanwhile, management highlighted expanding rate-reducing load efforts (4.6 GW in final engineering and 1.8 GW targeted online by 2030), plus Diablo Canyon licensing progress and CAISO transmission awards incorporated in the existing plan.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
